question: Explain the concept of a Turing machine and describe its role as the foundation of the theory of computation.
expectedPoints: A Turing machine is an abstract mathematical model consisting of an infinite tape, a read/write head, and a finite state control.
It can simulate the logic of any computer algorithm and is used to define the limits of what can be computed.
The Church-Turing thesis posits that any function that can be computed by an algorithm can be computed by a Turing machine, establishing it as a fundamental model for computability.

question: Define the Halting Problem and state why it is significant in computer science.
expectedPoints: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary program and an input, whether the program will finish running (halt) or continue to run forever.
Alan Turing proved that no general algorithm exists to solve the Halting Problem for all possible program-input pairs.
Its significance lies in being one of the first problems proven to be undecidable, demonstrating fundamental limits to what computer programs can do.

question: Compare and contrast the classes P, NP, and NP-complete. Discuss the implications of the P versus NP problem and name one classic NP-complete problem.
expectedPoints: P is the class of problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. NP is the class of problems verifiable in polynomial time, though not necessarily solvable in it.
NP-complete problems are the hardest problems in NP; if any NP-complete problem is in P, then P = NP. The P versus NP problem asks whether every problem in NP is also in P.
The resolution of P vs NP has profound implications for cryptography, optimization, and artificial intelligence. The Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is a classic NP-complete problem.
